Reconcile species names between two datasets

Source: R/reconcile_data.R
reconcile_data.Rd

Compares species names in two data frames and produces a reconciliation mapping documenting exact matches, normalised matches, synonym-based matches, and unresolved names.

Usage

reconcile_data(
  x,
  y,
  x_species = NULL,
  y_species = NULL,
  authority = "col",
  rank = c("species", "subspecies"),
  overrides = NULL,
  db_version = NULL,
  fuzzy = FALSE,
  fuzzy_threshold = 0.9,
  resolve = c("flag", "first"),
  quiet = FALSE
)

Arguments

x

A data frame (first dataset).

y

A data frame (second dataset).

x_species

Character(1). Column name in x containing species names. Auto-detected if NULL.

y_species

Character(1). Column name in y containing species names. Auto-detected if NULL.

authority

Character(1). Taxonomic authority for synonym resolution: "col" (default), "itis", "gbif", "ncbi", etc. Set to NULL to skip synonym lookup entirely.

rank

Character(1). "species" (default) strips infraspecific epithets during normalisation. "subspecies" retains trinomials.

overrides

A data frame with at least columns name_x and name_y containing pre-built name corrections, or a file path to a CSV. Optional column user_note for documentation. Set to NULL (default) for no overrides.

db_version

Character(1). taxadb database version. NULL uses the latest available.

fuzzy

Logical. Enable fuzzy matching for likely typos? Default FALSE. When TRUE, names that remain unmatched after synonym resolution are compared using component-based string similarity.

fuzzy_threshold

Numeric (0–1). Minimum similarity score for fuzzy matches. Default 0.9 (conservative; catches obvious typos).

resolve

Character(1). How to handle low-confidence matches: "flag" (default) marks fuzzy matches below 0.95 and indirect synonym matches as match_type = "flagged" for manual review. "first" accepts all matches at face value.

quiet

Logical. Suppress progress messages? Default FALSE.

Value

A reconciliation object. Use print() for a summary, reconcile_summary() for details, reconcile_mapping() to extract the mapping table, and reconcile_apply() to produce aligned data.

Examples

data(avonet_subset)
data(nesttrait_subset)
result <- reconcile_data(avonet_subset, nesttrait_subset,
                         x_species = "Species1",
                         y_species = "Scientific_name",
                         authority = NULL)
#> ℹ Reconciling 919 names (x) vs 916 names (y)
#> ℹ Matching 919 x 916 names through 2 stages...
#> ℹ Stage 1/4: Exact matching...
#> ℹ Stage 2/4: Normalised matching (916 matched so far)...
#> ℹ Stage 3/4: Synonym resolution (916 matched so far)...
#> ℹ Stage 4/4: Fuzzy matching (916 matched so far)...
#> ✔ Matched 916/919 names from x
print(result)
#> 
#> ── Reconciliation: data vs data ────────────────────────────────────────────────
#>   Source x: avonet_subset
#>   Source y: nesttrait_subset
#>   Authority: none
#>   Timestamp: 2026-04-06 15:46:32
#> ℹ Match coverage: [██████████████████████████████] 100% (916/919)
#> 
#> ── Match summary ──
#> 
#> • Exact: 916 (99.7%)
#> • Normalized: 0 ( 0.0%)
#> • Synonym: 0 ( 0.0%)
#> • Fuzzy: 0 ( 0.0%)
#> • Manual: 0 ( 0.0%)
#> ! Unresolved (x only):3 ( 0.3%)
#> ! Unresolved (y only):0
#> ! Flagged for review: 0
#> ℹ Use `reconcile_summary()` for details, `reconcile_mapping()` for the full table.
